summary_text:
## Court Name : Supreme Court of India
## Petitioner Name(s) : Mohammed Enterprises (Tanzania) Ltd.
## Respondent Name(s) : Farooq Ali Khan & Ors.

## Case Background: 
The case revolves around the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP) under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016. The petitioner, Mohammed Enterprises (Tanzania) Ltd., is challenging the High Court's decision to interdict CIRP proceedings.

## Petitioner Arguments:
The petitioner argues that the High Court erred in entertaining the writ petition, particularly due to delay and laches. They also argue that the High Court should have noted that the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code is a complete code with sufficient checks and balances, remedial avenues, and appeals.

## Respondent Argument:
The respondent, Farooq Ali Khan & Ors., argues that the High Court was justified in interdicting CIRP proceedings as principles of natural justice were violated. They also argue that they had availed statutory remedies under the Code by filing interlocutory applications seeking similar relief.

## Issues discussed in Court:
- Jurisdiction and power of the Adjudicating Authority under Section 60(5)(c)
- Principles of natural justice
- Delay and laches
- Sufficiency of checks and balances in the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code

## Final Decision:
The Supreme Court allows the appeals and sets aside the final judgment and order passed by the High Court.

## Court's Reasoning:
The court holds that the High Court committed an error in entertaining the writ petition due to delay and laches. They also note that the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code is a complete code with sufficient checks and balances, remedial avenues, and appeals.

## List of Important Precedents Cited:
- CoC of KSK Mahanadai Power Company Limited v. M/s UP Power Corporation Limited
- Whirlpool Corporation v. Registrar of Trade Marks, Mumbai and Ors.
- Committee of Creditors of Essar Steel India Ltd. v. Satish Kumar Gupta
- Gujarat Urja Vikas Nigam Limited v. Amit Gupta

## List of Acts:
- Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016

summary_text:
## Court Name: Supreme Court of India
## Petitioner Name(s): UCO Bank & Anr.
## Respondent Name(s): Vijay Kumar Handa
## Case Background: The respondent, Vijay Kumar Handa, was serving as a Clerk in the appellant bank when he was dismissed from service for indulging in riotous, disorderly and indecent behavior within the premises of the bank. He was found guilty of gross misconduct under Clause 19.5(c) of the Bipartite Settlement dated 19.10.1966.
## Petitioner Arguments: Learned senior counsel for the appellant argued that the respondent was not entitled to pension as he did not fulfill the requirements of pension in terms of the ninth Bipartite Settlement dated 27.04.2010, and Regulation 22 of the UCO Bank (Employees') Pension Regulations, 1995 does not permit grant of pension to delinquent employees.
## Respondent Argument: Learned counsel for the respondent relied on the decision of this Court in S.K. Kool (supra) and submitted that the respondent having completed the minimum pensionable years of service, he is entitled to the pensionary benefits.
## Issues discussed in Court: Whether the respondent was entitled to receive terminal benefits for the period of service he had rendered; whether the penalty of removal from service would be without disqualification from future employment; and whether the Labour Court's award substituting the penalty of stoppage of four increments for one year with further direction for reinstatement in service was justified.
## Final Decision: The appeal is dismissed.
## Court's Reasoning: The court relied on its previous decision in S.K. Kool (supra) and held that an employee who is found guilty of gross misconduct under Clause 19.5(c) of the Bipartite Settlement shall be entitled to superannuation benefits "as would be due otherwise" under the Regulations.
## List of Important Precedents Cited: Bank of Baroda v. S.K. Kool [2013] 12 SCR 783
## List of Acts:
- Industrial Disputes Act, 1947
- Industrial Disputes (Central) Rules, 1957
- UCO Bank (Employees') Pension Regulations, 1995

summary_text:
## Court Name: Supreme Court of India
## Petitioner Name(s): Shabeen Ahmad 
## Respondent Name(s): The State of Uttar Pradesh & Anr. 

## Case Background: 
The case pertains to the correctness of the order passed by the High Court granting bail to in-laws of the deceased wife, charged for causing dowry death of the deceased.

## Petitioner Arguments:
The Appellant (Shabeen Ahmad) has challenged the orders granting bail to the Respondent-accused, arguing that the High Court erred in overlooking substantial material indicating their involvement in the alleged offence.

## Respondent Argument: 
The Respondents (Accused Nos. 2, 3, 4, and 5) have argued that they should be granted bail as they have no prior criminal history, some of them are women, and certain co-accused had already been granted bail.

## Issues discussed in Court:
Stricter judicial scrutiny necessary in matters where a young woman loses her life in her matrimonial home under highly suspicious circumstances so soon after marriage, particularly where the record points to persistent harassment over unmet dowry demands.

## Final Decision: 
The bail granted to Accused No.2 (father-in-law) and Accused No.3 (mother-in-law) is hereby cancelled. The bail granted to Accused No.4 (Saba) and Accused No.5 (Ayasha) is upheld.

## Court's Reasoning:
The Court found that the Respondent-accused did not deserve continued protection of bail as the evidence indicated their direct involvement in the fatal events. However, leniency was shown towards Accused No.4 and Accused No.5 due to their personal and educational circumstances.

## List of Important Precedents Cited:
Ajwar v. Waseem [2024] 5 SCR 575 : (2024) 10 SCC 768

## List of Acts: 
Penal Code, 1860; Dowry Prohibition Act, 1961

summary_text:
## Court Name: Supreme Court of India

## Petitioner Name(s): Kalluri Naga Narasimha Abhiram & Ors. Etc.

## Respondent Name(s): The State of Telangana & Ors. Etc.

## Case Background: 
The case pertains to the validity of the amended Rules of 2017, which provide for reservation in admissions to medical colleges in the State of Telangana. The petitioners, who did not fall under the definition of "local candidates," challenged the rule on the grounds that it was exclusionary and violative of Article 14 of the Constitution.

## Petitioner Arguments: 
The petitioners argued that the amended rule was arbitrary and unconstitutional as it excluded students who studied outside Telangana but had permanent residence or domicile in the State. They also claimed that the rule created an unintelligible differentia between students based on their acquiring qualifications from schools within the Municipal limits or AUDA.

## Respondent Argument: 
The State of Telangana argued that the amended rule was permissible as it provided for a "Competent Authority Quota" and traced its power to the Regulation Act of 1983, which had been brought out under Entry 25 of List III, Seventh Schedule read with Article 371D and the Presidential Order of 1974.

## Issues discussed in Court: 
The court discussed the validity of the amended rule, whether it was exclusionary, arbitrary, and violative of Article 14. The court also considered the principle of reading down and whether the amended rule had to be struck down or read down.

## Final Decision: 
The Supreme Court upheld the Rules of 2017 as amended in 2024, with a proviso to Rule 3 that would allay the grievances of students who were taken out of the State by compulsion of their parents' employment. The court allowed the appeals of the State and the University.

## Court's Reasoning: 
The court relied on previous decisions such as Pradeep Jain v. Union of India, Anand Madaan v. State of Haryana, and Rajdeep Ghosh v. State of Assam, which had laid down that the requirement of residence/domicile for admission to MBBS/BDS courses was permissible.

## List of Important Precedents Cited: 
- Pradeep Jain v. Union of India
- Anand Madaan v. State of Haryana
- Rajdeep Ghosh v. State of Assam

## List of Acts: 
- The Reorganisation Act of Andhra Pradesh
- Regulation Act of 1983

Court on two grounds. It was held that mere likelihood of hardship 
cannot result in the striking down of a rule and in any event, hardship 
is likely to arise in the working of almost any rule, especially when 
applied to a selection of a limited number of candidates, which alone 
cannot render the rule unconstitutional. It was clearly held that, for 
relief against such hardship and reducing the wide gap between 
the number of available seats and the number of aspirants, the 
grievance would have to be addressed elsewhere; clearly indicating 
the policy formulation by the government/legislature. The exclusive 
domain for policy formulation was not liable to be interfered with, 
unless validly challenged on gross discrimination, clear arbitrariness, 
patent illegality, perversity or unconstitutionality.

23. Having found so, the Court went to the further question as to 
whether the rule defining a local candidate is arbitrary and violative 
of Article 14 of the Constitution of India. Relying upon the decisions 
in Ahmedabad Municipal Corpn. v. Nilaybhai R. Thakore12 and 
Meenakshi Malik v. University of Delhi & Ors.13 as also decisions 
of various High Courts, it was found to be violative of the mandate 
contained in Article 14 of the Constitution. Relying on the trite principle 
that when a harmonious construction is possible, no provision of 
a statute or legislation should be struck down, the Division Bench 
thus expanded the definition to include any student who produced 
his residence certificate issued by a competent authority of the 
Government of Telangana. At the outset, we have to state that 
without a definition of what constitutes residence or at least without 
reference to a statute or rule prescribing the issuance of a residence 
certificate, the directions issued by the High Court would only result 
in an anomalous situation, making the reservation unworkable and 
open to a series of litigation. 
24. Yet again, as has been argued by the State and the University, similar 
provisions have been upheld by this Court in a number of decisions 
over very many years. D.P.Joshi v. State of Madhya Bharat and 
Ors.14 upheld the levy of capitation fee on those students residing 
outside Madhya Bharat. The object of the classification, found to 
be justified, was the State’s desire to help at least to some extent 
the students who are its residents, encouraging education of the

The State counters that the definition has been molded in such a 
manner as to not only benefit those people who studied and resided 
for a considerable period within the State; but also ensure that those 
students who come from the marginalized sections are included. Such 
persons are those who are born into families who do not have the

these are the persons who would remain within the State and offer

summary_text:
## Court Name:
Supreme Court of India

## Petitioner Name(s):
Mahanadi Coal Fields Ltd. & Anr.

## Respondent Name(s):
Mathias Oram & Ors.

## Case Background: 
The case revolves around the acquisition of land by Mahanadi Coal Fields Ltd. (MCL) for its coal mining operations in various villages of Odisha, resulting in displacement of landowners and their families. The petitioners, Mathias Oram and others, have challenged the compensation and rehabilitation measures provided by MCL.

## Petitioner Arguments:
The petitioners have argued that the compensation and rehabilitation measures provided by MCL are inadequate and do not fully address their concerns. They have also sought directions from the Supreme Court to ensure that the land acquired is developed as per the Third Schedule of the Rehabilitation and Resettlement Policy, 2013.

## Respondent Argument:
MCL has argued that it has already made payments towards compensation for structures and has fulfilled its obligations under the Rehabilitation and Resettlement Policy, 2013. They have also sought to transfer the issue of Method of calculation adopted by the Commission to the High Court of Orissa.

## Issues discussed in Court:
The Supreme Court has discussed various issues including the application of the Gopalpur Model for determining compensation, the eligibility of landowners and their families for employment benefits, the suitability of resettlement sites, and the payment of one-time compensation to displaced families.

## Final Decision:
The Supreme Court has directed that the Commission should complete its task and finalize reports for villages Kiripsira, Ratansara, Jhupuranga, and Tumulia. It has also directed that the High Court shall decide the Writ Petition (C) being No. 39185/2023 regarding the Method of calculation adopted by the Commission.

## Court's Reasoning:
The Supreme Court has relied on its previous judgments and orders to arrive at its decision. It has held that the First Schedule of the Rehabilitation and Resettlement Policy, 2013 is applicable to the acquisition in question, made by MCL, in respect of villages where reports were not approved prior to 28.10.2015.

## List of Important Precedents Cited:
Not Available

## List of Acts:
Rehabilitation and Resettlement Policy, 2013

summary_text:
## Court Name: High Court of Judicature at Madras

## Petitioner Name(s): Chief Revenue Controlling Officer Cum Inspector General of Registration, & Ors.

## Respondent Name(s): P. Babu

## Case Background: The case involves a dispute over the market value of two properties, which were registered as documents under the Indian Stamp Act, 1899. The petitioner, Chief Revenue Controlling Officer Cum Inspector General of Registration, & Ors., has challenged the determination of the market value of the properties by the Collector (Stamps).

## Petitioner Arguments: The petitioner argues that the Registering Officer did not provide any reasons for concluding that the sale consideration shown in the sale deeds was undervalued.

## Respondent Argument: The respondent, P. Babu, has filed objections against the determination of the market value of the properties by the Collector (Stamps).

## Issues discussed in Court: The court discussed the issues of whether the Registering Officer had the power to refer the matter to the Collector for determining the market value of the properties, and whether the Collector's decision was correct.

## Final Decision: The High Court dismissed the appeals filed by the petitioner.

## Court's Reasoning: The court held that the Registering Officer did not provide any reasons for concluding that the sale consideration shown in the sale deeds was undervalued, and therefore, the Collector's decision was not justified. The court also held that the Collector had the power to determine the market value of the properties under Section 47-A of the Indian Stamp Act, 1899.

## List of Important Precedents Cited: G. Karmegnam v. The Joint Sub-Registrar, Madurai

## List of Acts: Indian Stamp Act, 1899

summary_text:
## Court Name:
Supreme Court of India

## Petitioner Name(s):
Gulshan Kumar

## Respondent Name(s):
Institute of Banking Personnel Selection & Ors.

## Case Background:
The petitioner, Gulshan Kumar, filed a writ petition against the Institute of Banking Personnel Selection & Ors., alleging that they had not been providing reasonable accommodations to persons with disabilities (PwD) candidates in their examinations. The petitioner claimed that the Respondent No. 5's Office Memorandum dated 10.08.2022 failed to extend benefits to PwD candidates, despite the directions of the Supreme Court.

## Petitioner Arguments:
The petitioner argued that the Office Memorandum did not provide for facilities other than a scribe and compensatory time, which was contrary to the provisions of the RPwD Act, 2016. The petitioner also claimed that the absence of a clear grievance redressal mechanism caused inconvenience and injustice to PwD candidates.

## Respondent Argument:
The respondents stated that they had been following the directions of the Supreme Court and the guidelines issued by Respondent No. 5 in the Office Memorandum dated 10.08.2022.

## Issues discussed in Court:
The court discussed the issues of reasonable accommodation, discrimination against PwD candidates, and the need for a uniform memorandum for examinations applicable to all PwD candidates.

## Final Decision:
The Supreme Court directed the Respondent No. 5 to revisit the Office Memorandum dated 10.08.2022, remove the restrictions, and grant relaxations in a reasonable manner. The court also ordered the Respondent No. 5 to ensure strict compliance with the guidelines issued by the Supreme Court.

## Court's Reasoning:
The court reasoned that the rights under Articles 19 and 21 of the Constitution can be enforced even against private entities and that the benefits conferred by the statute should be provided for all PwD candidates.

## List of Important Precedents Cited:
Not Available

## List of Acts:
RPwD Act, 2016

chunks.chunk_content:
in Kaushal Kishor v. State of Uttar Pradesh and others15 considered 
the question as to whether fundamental rights under Articles 19 and 
21 of the Constitution can be claimed against anyone who is not a 
state instrumentality, and answered the same in the affirmative with 
a majority of 4:1. It was clarified that rights under Articles 19 and 
21 can be enforced even against private entities and it overrides 
the principle laid down in Rajbir (supra). Hence, the contention of 
the Respondent No.1 that they are not amenable to writ jurisdiction 
cannot be countenanced by us. It is noteworthy mentioning that the 
Office Memorandum clearly stated that the guidelines are applicable 
to all the authorities. As such, the benefits conferred by the statute 
should be provided for all the PwD candidates and they cannot be 
denied on the ground of absence of accountability and/or lack of 
duty on enforceability.
19. In the ultimate analysis, we are of the considered view that the 
guidelines issued by the Respondent No.5 pursuant to the directions 
of this Court, have to be enforced, by extending the benefits for PwBD 
candidates to all PwD candidates in writing their examinations, without 
any hindrance. Accordingly, we direct the Respondent No.5 to revisit 
the Office Memorandum dated 10.08.2022, remove the restrictions 
and grant relaxations in a reasonable manner and incorporate the 
following aspects and thereby, re-notify the same afresh, within a 
period of two months: 
(i) 
direct all the authorities / recruitment agencies / examining bodies 
to uniformly follow the guidelines issued by the Respondent 
No.5, which is the nodal agency and ensure strict adherence 
through periodic surveys / verification;
(ii) 
carry out periodic sensitization drive at educational institutions 
to raise awareness among the examination conducting bodies 
so as to ensure that the OMs are effectively implemented;
(iii) set up a grievance redressal portal to register complaints, 
which would permit the candidates to approach it first before 
approaching the court of law;
(iv) inspect the guidelines framed by different authorities and re-
notify the existing guidelines with an aim to ensure compliance; 
15

342 
(v) 
extend the validity of the scribe certificate (currently being valid 
only for 6 months) to prevent the long wait time after applying, 
especially, in rural areas;

summary_text:
## Court Name: Supreme Court of India

## Petitioner Name(s): K.R. Suresh

## Respondent Name(s): R. Poornima & Ors.

## Case Background: The case revolves around a dispute between the petitioner, K.R. Suresh, and the respondents, R. Poornima & Ors., regarding the forfeiture of advance money paid by the petitioner to the respondents.

## Petitioner Arguments: The petitioner argued that the respondents had forfeited the advance money without justification and that they were entitled to a refund under Section 22 of the 1963 Act.

## Respondent Argument: The respondents argued that the petitioner had not claimed an alternative prayer for refund of earnest money in the suit and that the court could not grant such relief suo moto.

## Issues discussed in Court: The court discussed the interpretation of Section 22 of the 1963 Act, particularly Sub-section (2), and whether it is mandatory or directory in nature.

## Final Decision: The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, holding that the respondents had justified the forfeiture of advance money and that the petitioner was not entitled to a refund under Section 22 of the 1963 Act.

## Court's Reasoning: The court relied on precedents such as Desh Raj v. Rohtash Singh and Kamal Kumar v. Premlata Joshi, which established that the relief of refund of earnest money must be specifically claimed in the plaint or by way of an amendment.

## List of Important Precedents Cited:

- Desh Raj v. Rohtash Singh
- Kamal Kumar v. Premlata Joshi

## List of Acts: 

- 1963 Act

## Court Name : Supreme Court of India
## Petitioner Name(s) : Daljit Singh
## Respondent Name(s) : State of Haryana & Anr.

## Case Background: 
The case revolves around a complaint filed by the NHAI (National Highways Authority of India) against the Appellant, Daljit Singh, for allegedly failing to meet specifications in a contract for '8-Laning' of a National Highway. The dispute led to the filing of a complaint, summoning order, and an order declaring the Appellant a proclaimed offender.

## Petitioner Arguments: 
The Appellant argued that his status as a proclaimed offender should be nullified if he stands acquitted during trial in connection with the very same offence. He also claimed that the subsistence of the proclamation under Section 82 Cr.P.C. is necessary for the authorities to proceed against an accused against whom such a proclamation stands issued, under Section 174A IPC.

## Respondent Argument: 
The State of Haryana & Anr. argued that the prosecution can still proceed against the Appellant for having not appeared before a Court during the time that the process was in effect, even if the status u/s.82 Cr.P.C. is nullified.

## Issues discussed in Court:
Whether the proclaimed offender status, under the provisions of the Cr.P.C., of an accused can subsist if such accused stands acquitted during trial in connection to the very same offence? Whether the subsistence of the proclamation u/s 82 Cr.P.C. is necessary for the authorities to proceed against the accused person u/s 174A IPC?

## Final Decision: 
The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, holding that Section 174A IPC is an independent, substantive offence that can continue even if the proclamation under Section 82, Cr.P.C. is extinguished.

## Court's Reasoning: 
The court held that while proceedings under Section 174A IPC cannot be initiated independent of Section 82, Cr.P.C., they can continue if the said proclamation is no longer in effect. The court also noted that the Appellant has been acquitted, meaning there is no case for which his presence is required to be secured.

## List of Important Precedents Cited: 
Mukesh Bhatia v. State (NCT of Delhi), Divya Verma v. State, Sameena & Anr. v. State GNCT of Delhi & Anr., Kartarey v. State of U.P., Jayendra Vishnu Thakur v. State of Maharashtra, Vimlaben Ajitbhai Patel v. Vatslaben Ashok bhai Patel.

## List of Acts: 
Penal Code, 1860; Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973; Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881

## Court Name : "Supreme Court of India"
## Petitioner Name(s) : Serosoft Solutions Pvt. Ltd.
## Respondent Name(s) : Dexter Capital Advisors Pvt. Ltd.

## Case Background: 
The case revolves around a dispute between Serosoft Solutions Pvt. Ltd. and Dexter Capital Advisors Pvt. Ltd., regarding the non-payment of fees for services rendered by Dexter Capital Advisors Pvt. Ltd. to Serosoft Solutions Pvt. Ltd. The dispute was resolved through arbitration, with the Arbitral Tribunal rejecting the respondent/claimant's request for further opportunity to cross-examine one of its witnesses.

## Petitioner Arguments: 
The petitioner argued that the High Court should have restrained itself from interfering with the directions of the Arbitral Tribunal holding that full and sufficient opportunity to cross-examine RW-1 has already been given and no further extension of time is warranted.

## Respondent Argument: 
The respondent/claimant argued that they were unsatisfied with the initial cross-examination of their witness RW-1, which had exceeded 12 hours, and sought a further opportunity to cross-examine. The High Court granted this request, despite the Arbitral Tribunal's rejection.

## Issues discussed in Court:
* Whether the High Court correctly exercised its supervisory jurisdiction under Article 227 in granting the respondent/claimant one more opportunity to cross-examine appellant/respondent’s witness.
* The correct interpretation of Section 29A of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.

## Final Decision: 
The Supreme Court allowed the appeals and set aside the orders passed by the High Court in CM(M) 3711/2004 and CM Appl. 63047/2024 dated 25.10.2024.

## Court's Reasoning:
* The court held that the Arbitral Tribunal had given full opportunity to all parties, and the High Court should have restrained itself from interfering.
* The court relied on a judgment of the same Court (Kelvin Air Conditioning and Ventilation System Pvt. Ltd. v. Triumph Reality Pvt. Ltd.) which mentioned conditions for exercising jurisdiction under Articles 226/227.

## List of Important Precedents Cited:
Kelvin Air Conditioning and Ventilation System Pvt. Ltd. v. Triumph Reality Pvt. Ltd.

## List of Acts:
* Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996
* Constitution of India

viability of the society, allowing the registration of such a society 
which might not even be able to function, it may adversely affect 
the members of the society and, ultimately, it would be frustrating 
the very object of the establishment of the said society.
31. It must be noted that the constitution of the Committee and the 
examination of the proposal by the Committee is a vital part of the 
Policy Directives of the State Government which is required to be 
complied with conditions under Section 4 of the 1960 Act. Therefore, 
ignoring the findings of the Committee and allowing the registration 
of the society when the appellants have been unable to point out 
any perversity in the said findings shall lead to an unjustifiable 
interference in the Committee’s Order.
32. Further, it must be noted that the State Government may use its 
discretion for relaxation of conditions. However, such a discretion 
424 
Supreme Court Reports
cannot be used to frustrate the very object of the Act. Such a power 
of relaxing the necessary pre-requisites could have been made 
only through the means of a Government Resolution and not at the 
whims of the State in an appeal which essentially led to by-passing 
the eligibility criteria set out by the Government through its multiple 
Resolutions. Once such an eligibility standard has been set out by 
the Government, the only proper route to introduce any alteration or 
relaxation of these conditions would have been through a subsequent 
Government Resolution. In the present case, by relaxing the pre-
requisite condition relating to financial viability, the State allowed the 
registration of the society, which in our opinion, resulted in nothing 
but frustrating the very object of the Act.
33. Lastly, with respect to the locus standi of the respondent no. 6, such 
an argument by the appellants shall not restrain interference by this 
Court or the High Court in the matter when there is patent illegality 
in the State’s order which requires interference by the Courts of law.
34. Therefore, considering all these aspects, we are unable to find 
any fault in the order passed by the High Court. The High Court of 
Judicature at Bombay has rightly allowed the petition and set aside 
the order passed by the State.
35. Accordingly, the appeals stand dismissed, and the impugned order 
of the High Court is upheld.
36. Pending application(s), if any, shall be disposed of accordingly.
37. No order as to cost.

Judge as their petitions had been dismissed but still, they had been

before the Division Bench. This objection was taken only to delay the

ought not to have benefitted out of the technical objection raised by 
them regarding the limitations when they were themselves bound by 
the decision of the learned Single Judge and had no other objection 
or denial to the demand except that of the pending appeals before

18. In the above facts and circumstances, we are not entering into the 
other arguments advanced by the parties. We are restoring the writ 
petition of the appellants to be heard after the decision in the intra-court 
appeals. In case the appeals are allowed by the Division Bench then 
there would no question of any recovery retrospectively. The demands 
would be liable to be withdrawn. However, if the respondents fail in 
their appeals, they would be liable to pay the demand in accordance 
to law along with interest admissible under law. 
19. We accordingly allow the appeals, set aside the impugned order of 
the High Court and restore the writ petitions before the High Court 
to be heard after disposal of the pending intra-court appeals filed 
by the respondents.
Result of the case: Appeals allowed.

17. The decision in Ranjan Kumar (supra) was followed by the decision 
in Kulwant Singh v. Dayaram,18 where promotion to the post of 
head constables in Chandigarh Police was the subject matter of 
consideration. This Court held that after appearing in a competitive 
examination and upon being selected, the appointees become an 
identified category and that if the rights of such appointees forming part 
of such identified category are to be affected by any determination, the 
situation commands that they should be impleaded in the proceedings 
as necessary parties. The non-joinder now permits them to take the 
plea that the impugned order does not bind them. 
18. The decisions in Ranjan Kumar (supra) and Kulwant Singh (supra) 
are authorities for the proposition that selectees who are appointed 
or promoted must be arrayed as parties in the original proceedings 
where their selections are challenged. Kulwant Singh (supra) has

of reassessment even where the original period for assessment has 
expired; and vi) the proviso to the amended section 29(4) is contrary

5. 
The High Court, vide the judgment dated 07.08.2015 in Amrit 
Banaspati, observed that the amendment cannot by any stretch of 
imagination be held to be so unreasonable or excessive as to warrant 
it being declared invalid. On a survey of cases, it highlighted that 
the principle was that the proceedings which have attained finality 
under the existing law due to a bar of limitation cannot be held to 
be open for revival unless the amended provision is clearly given 
a retrospective operation so as to allow unsettling of proceedings, 
which had already been concluded and attained finality. That the 
purpose and effect of the entire amendment was to obviate the 
consequences of the proviso to the unamended section. Following 
the above judgment, the High Court dismissed the writ petitions filed 
by the appellants herein.

24. Accordingly, for reasons aforesaid, the appeal is allowed. The 
Impugned Order is set aside. Though the natural consequence 
would entail revival of the conviction and sentence imposed upon 
the respondent no.2 i.e., one year simple imprisonment and fine of 
Rs.35,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Five Lakhs), but having regard to 
the parting submissions of learned senior counsel for the accused/
respondent no.2, to the effect that considering his age, he may be 
520 
only subjected to fine and not imprisonment, we are inclined to modify 
the sentence to only payment of a fine restricted to Rs.32,00,000/- 
(Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs). Acceding to the request by the learned 
senior counsel, such fine be paid within four months from today to 
the appellant, failing which the sentence in entirety, as awarded by 
the Trial Court and upheld by the Appellate Court, will stand restored, 
with the added modification that the entire fine of Rs.35,00,000/- 
(Rupees Thirty-Five Lakhs) will be payable to the appellant. 
25. Parties are left to bear their own costs. I.A. No.99358/2024 (exemption 
from filing Official Translation) is allowed. I.A. No.234705/2024 (to 
file additional documents) is allowed; Annexure A-1 (Application/
Tehrir) is taken on record.
Result of the case: Appeal allowed.
